advantages: Jacobian organizes all first-order sensitivities of vector-valued maps in one matrix—essential for the multivariable chain rule and backpropagation.
Hessian captures curvature beyond gradient direction, enabling second-order optimizers that can converge faster on well-conditioned losses.
Hessian eigenvalues at critical points classify local minima, maxima, and saddles on loss surfaces.
Jacobian determinants support exact density transforms in normalizing flows and change-of-variables formulas.
Vector-Jacobian products propagate gradients efficiently without materializing huge Jacobians for wide layers.
disadvantages: Full Jacobian for f : ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ has mn entries—expensive to store when m and n are large (e.g., every pixel output vs every weight).
Full Hessian for p parameters requires O(p²) storage and O(p³) inversion—impractical for modern neural networks.
Hessian information is local; positive definiteness at one point does not guarantee a global minimum on non-convex losses.
Non-smooth activations (ReLU) yield subgradient Jacobians and ill-defined classical second derivatives at kinks.
Numerical Hessians via finite differences or double autograd accumulate floating-point error and can be unstable.
